Description: Optimal average power for Terracotta surface cleaning without thermal damage
Near-infrared wavelength optimized for Terracotta absorption and minimal substrate damage
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control and adequate energy density
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and thermal management
Fluence level sufficient for contaminant removal while preserving Terracotta substrate
Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant removal with controlled thermal penetration
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning and process efficiency
Recommended number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage without excessive thermal buildup
Effective dwell time per spot for contaminant ablation without substrate damage

Typical range for delicate ceramics like terracotta to avoid thermal damage; starting at 50W for light contamination removal. Max safe ~200W to prevent cracking. Source: Studies on Nd:YAG laser cleaning of archaeological ceramics (e.g., Verges-Belmin et al., 2008, J. Cult. Herit.).

Effective speeds balance cleaning efficiency and heat input; slower for heavy contamination (500-1000 mm/s), faster for light (2000-3000 mm/s). Recommended 1500 mm/s for uniform terracotta surface preparation. Source: Industry manuals from IPG Photonics and Laserax equipment guidelines.

Nanosecond regime preferred for terracotta to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ) while ablating contaminants; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precision but less common industrially. 15 ns optimal for low thermal conductivity materials. Source: Laser ablation research (e.g., Anisimov et al., 2015, Appl. Phys. A).

Typical for 1064 nm cleaning to achieve good overlap without excessive heat buildup; higher rates speed up process but risk accumulation on porous terracotta. 20 kHz balances speed and safety. Source: Academic literature on pulsed laser cleaning (e.g., Pouli et al., 2010, Spectrochim. Acta B).

Fluence range for contaminant ablation without substrate damage; terracotta damage threshold ~2-4 J/cm² due to low melting point (~1000-1200°C). Recommended 1 J/cm² for safe industrial cleaning. Source: Ablation threshold studies on ceramics (e.g., Marla et al., 2012, J. Appl. Phys.).

Smaller spots for fine detail on terracotta artifacts, larger for broad industrial areas; 200 μm recommended for balanced resolution and coverage on uneven surfaces. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems.

Single pass for light soiling, multi-pass for heavy contamination; 2 passes typical for complete terracotta cleaning without overexposure. Source: Case studies in cultural heritage laser cleaning (e.g., Radvan et al., 2007, Lasers in the Conservation of Artworks).
Ensures uniform coverage on porous terracotta; 70% optimal to avoid gaps or uneven heating. Source: Process optimization in laser scanning studies (e.g., Salonitis et al., 2013, Opt. Lasers Eng.).
